preamble: The shift from the labiodental fricative [f] to the vowel [u] is a form of vocalization. It typically occurs in coda positions where the fricative weakens into a labio-velar glide [w] before merging into a back rounded vowel.

examples.note: OE intervocalic /f/ realized as [v] → vocalized to [w/u]: hafoc → hawc → hawk. Note: the vocalizing segment is [v], not [f]; f→u path goes through v→w→u.
OF labial [v] before consonant vocalized to [u]: deveir → devoir → dû (had to). Again the vocalizing segment is [v], not [f] directly.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
Baugh, A. C. & Cable, T. (2013). A History of the English Language (6th ed.). Routledge.
